summary: This notebook implements a minimal submission strategy for an LLM evaluation competition by generating prompts designed to manipulate a judge model's scoring behavior. It randomly splits the test set into two groups: one receives a direct prompt injection instructing the model to assign a score of 9, while the other receives a multiple-choice prompt asking the model to select a summary. The approach demonstrates how simple prompt engineering and instruction overrides can serve as a functional baseline without training any models. Readers should understand that LLM judges can be vulnerable to direct command injection and that randomized prompt mixing is a common tactic in adversarial submissions.
competition_flow: Loads test topics and a word list, randomly splits the dataset into two groups, generates prompt injection and multiple-choice prompts for each, and exports the combined prompts as a CSV submission.
data_reading: Loads test data and sample submission via pandas CSV readers.
Reads a local word list file using Python's built-in file I/O.
data_processing: Applies string formatting to construct prompts.
Randomly shuffles test indices and partitions them into two groups.
insights: Simple prompt injection can reliably override an LLM evaluation criteria when direct instructions are provided.
Randomly partitioning prompts may help evade detection or exploit judge model inconsistencies.
Zero-shot prompt engineering is sufficient to generate a valid baseline submission for LLM-based competitions.
